lessons.title: Charge, Current, Voltage, Power & Sources
lessons.content:
Analog electronics is written in the algebra of **charge, current, voltage, and power**, so getting these definitions reflexive is the whole game. Current is the flow rate of charge, $i = \frac{dq}{dt}$, measured in amperes ($1\,\text{A} = 1\,\text{C/s}$). Voltage is energy per unit charge, $v = \frac{dw}{dq}$, measured in volts ($1\,\text{V} = 1\,\text{J/C}$). Instantaneous power follows directly: $p = vi$, in watts.

The bookkeeping that keeps signs honest is the **passive sign convention** (PSC): draw the current *entering* the element at its **positive** voltage terminal, then $p = vi$. With this convention, $p > 0$ means the element *absorbs* power (resistors always do) and $p < 0$ means it *delivers* power (what an active source does). Conservation guarantees that, summed over a complete circuit, supplied power equals absorbed power.

We idealize energy delivery with two source types. An **ideal independent voltage source** holds its terminal voltage fixed regardless of the current drawn; an **ideal independent current source** forces a fixed current regardless of the voltage across it. **Dependent (controlled) sources** — voltage- or current-controlled — produce an output proportional to some other circuit variable and are the modeling backbone of transistors and op-amps later in this module.

Real sources are not ideal: a battery behaves as an ideal source in series with a small **internal resistance**, which is exactly why terminal voltage sags under load. Holding these conventions firmly now prevents the classic rust-knocker sign errors that creep into everything downstream.

items.front: Define electric current and give its unit and defining equation.
items.back: Current is the rate of flow of charge: $i = \frac{dq}{dt}$. Unit is the ampere (A), where $1\,\text{A} = 1\,\text{C/s}$. Conventional current flows in the direction of positive-charge motion (opposite to electron flow).

items.front: Define voltage (potential difference) in terms of energy and charge.
items.back: Voltage is energy per unit charge: $v = \frac{dw}{dq}$. Unit is the volt (V), where $1\,\text{V} = 1\,\text{J/C}$. It is the work per unit positive charge moved between two points.

items.front: State the passive sign convention and what positive power means under it.
items.back: Assign current entering the element at its positive voltage terminal; then $p = vi$. If $p > 0$ the element absorbs power; if $p < 0$ it delivers power. Resistors always absorb; sources usually deliver.

items.front: What distinguishes an ideal independent voltage source from an ideal independent current source?
items.back: An ideal voltage source maintains a fixed terminal voltage for any current drawn. An ideal current source forces a fixed current for any voltage across it. Each is an idealization with infinite/zero internal resistance respectively.
items.hint: One fixes V, the other fixes I.

items.front: What is a dependent (controlled) source and why does it matter?
items.back: A source whose output is proportional to another circuit variable (VCVS, VCCS, CCVS, CCCS). They model active devices: a BJT's collector current is essentially a current-controlled current source; an op-amp output is a voltage-controlled voltage source.
items.hint: Output set by another node's V or I.

items.front: Why does a real battery's terminal voltage drop under load?
items.back: A real source is an ideal source in series with internal resistance $R_{int}$. Drawing current $I$ drops $I R_{int}$ inside, so the terminal voltage is $V_{term} = V_{oc} - I R_{int}$. Higher current means more sag.

items.prompt: A 12 V battery with $0.5\,\Omega$ internal resistance delivers 4 A to a load. What is the terminal voltage?
items.explanation: $V_{term} = V_{oc} - I R_{int} = 12 - 4 \times 0.5 = 10\,\text{V}$. The 2 V difference is dropped across the internal resistance.

items.prompt: Under the passive sign convention, an element shows $v = +5\,\text{V}$ and $i = -2\,\text{A}$ (current measured into the + terminal). The element is:
items.explanation: $p = vi = 5 \times (-2) = -10\,\text{W}$. Negative power under the PSC means the element delivers (supplies) 10 W to the rest of the circuit.

items.prompt: Which statement about an ideal current source is correct?
choices: Its voltage is fixed and current adjusts
Its current is fixed and the voltage across it is whatever the circuit demands
It always has zero voltage across it
It can only source current into a short circuit
items.explanation: An ideal current source forces a constant current; the voltage across it is set by the external network and can be any value, including large voltages into a high resistance.

lessons.title: Ohm's Law, Power & Kirchhoff's Laws
lessons.content:
Three relations carry most of DC circuit analysis. **Ohm's law** links voltage and current in a resistor: $v = iR$, where resistance $R$ (ohms, $\Omega$) is the proportionality constant and conductance is its reciprocal $G = \frac{1}{R}$ (siemens). Resistance of a uniform conductor is $R = \rho \frac{\ell}{A}$, tying it to material **resistivity** $\rho$ and geometry; real resistors also carry a tolerance, power rating, and temperature coefficient you must respect.

Power dissipated in a resistor has three interchangeable forms — drill all of them:

$$P = VI = I^2 R = \frac{V^2}{R}$$

Use whichever pair of quantities you already know. Sizing a resistor's wattage from $I^2R$ is a daily back-of-envelope task.

**Kirchhoff's laws** are conservation statements that turn topology into equations. **KCL** (charge conservation): the algebraic sum of currents into any node is zero, $\sum i = 0$. **KVL** (energy conservation): the algebraic sum of voltages around any closed loop is zero, $\sum v = 0$. Together with Ohm's law they are sufficient to solve *any* linear resistive network, and they are the foundation under nodal and mesh analysis.

The **Wheatstone bridge** is a classic KCL/KVL application: four resistors in two divider legs, with balance (zero detector current) when $\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{R_3}{R_4}$. It is still the standard way to read strain gauges and resistive sensors, because measuring a small *difference* from balance beats measuring a large absolute resistance.

items.front: State Ohm's law and define conductance.
items.back: $v = iR$, where $R$ is resistance in ohms ($\Omega$). Conductance is the reciprocal, $G = \frac{1}{R}$, in siemens (S), so $i = Gv$.

items.front: Give the three equivalent forms of resistor power dissipation.
items.back: $P = VI = I^2 R = \frac{V^2}{R}$. Pick the form matching the quantities you know; $I^2R$ is the usual choice for sizing wattage.

items.front: State Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) and the conservation principle behind it.
items.back: The algebraic sum of currents entering any node is zero: $\sum i = 0$. It expresses conservation of charge — charge cannot accumulate at a node.

items.front: State Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) and its conservation basis.
items.back: The algebraic sum of voltages around any closed loop is zero: $\sum v = 0$. It expresses conservation of energy — net work moving a charge around a loop is zero.

items.front: How does resistance depend on material and geometry?
items.back: $R = \rho \frac{\ell}{A}$, where $\rho$ is resistivity ($\Omega\cdot\text{m}$), $\ell$ is length, and $A$ is cross-sectional area. Longer/thinner means more resistance.

items.front: What is the Wheatstone bridge balance condition?
items.back: With two divider legs $R_1/R_2$ and $R_3/R_4$, the bridge is balanced (zero detector current) when $\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{R_3}{R_4}$. Used to read strain gauges and resistive sensors as a small deviation from balance.
items.hint: Ratio of the two legs equal.

items.prompt: A $220\,\Omega$ resistor carries 50 mA. What power must it dissipate, and is a 1/4 W part adequate?
items.explanation: $P = I^2 R = (0.05)^2 \times 220 = 0.0025 \times 220 = 0.55\,\text{W}$. That exceeds 1/4 W (0.25 W), so a higher-rated resistor is required.

items.prompt: At a node, three currents enter: 2 A, 3 A, and an unknown $I_x$ leaves. A fourth branch of 4 A leaves. If 2 A and 3 A enter and 4 A leaves, what is $I_x$ (leaving)?
items.explanation: KCL: currents in equal currents out. $2 + 3 = 4 + I_x$, so $I_x = 5 - 4 = 1\,\text{A}$ leaving the node.

items.prompt: A Wheatstone bridge has $R_1 = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_3 = 1.5\,\text{k}\Omega$. What $R_4$ balances the bridge?

lessons.title: Series/Parallel Reduction, Dividers & Delta-Wye
lessons.content:
Most networks collapse with a handful of reduction rules you should know cold. **Series** resistors add: $R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2 + \cdots$. **Parallel** resistors combine by reciprocals, $\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \sum \frac{1}{R_i}$; for two in parallel the product-over-sum form is fastest:

$$R_{eq} = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

The **voltage divider** is the most-used circuit in analog design. For two series resistors across $V_{in}$, the voltage across $R_2$ is

$$V_{out} = V_{in}\,\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}.$$

Its dual, the **current divider**, splits a current between two parallel branches inversely to their resistance: $I_1 = I\,\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ (the *opposite* resistor appears on top).

The catch every rusty engineer rediscovers is **loading**: the unloaded divider equation only holds when whatever you connect to $V_{out}$ draws negligible current. A finite load $R_L$ appears in parallel with $R_2$, lowering the effective bottom resistance and pulling $V_{out}$ below the ideal prediction. This is the same effect that makes a $1\,\text{M}\Omega$ DMM perturb a high-impedance node.

When resistors form a bridge-like mesh that is neither purely series nor parallel, the **Delta-Wye** ($\Delta$-Y, or $\pi$-T) transformation rewrites three resistors in a triangle as three in a star (and back), unlocking further reduction. A Y resistor equals the product of its two adjacent $\Delta$ resistors over the sum of all three.

items.front: Give the voltage-divider formula for $V_{out}$ across $R_2$ in a two-resistor series string driven by $V_{in}$.
items.back: $V_{out} = V_{in}\,\dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$. The resistor you measure across appears in the numerator.

items.front: Give the two-resistor parallel combination formula.
items.back: $R_{eq} = \dfrac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ (product over sum). For $N$ resistors, $\dfrac{1}{R_{eq}} = \sum_i \dfrac{1}{R_i}$. The result is always smaller than the smallest resistor.

items.front: State the current-divider rule for two parallel resistors carrying total current $I$.
items.back: $I_1 = I\,\dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ and $I_2 = I\,\dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$. Note the OPPOSITE resistor appears in the numerator — more current goes through the smaller resistor.

items.front: What is the loading effect on a voltage divider, and how does a load $R_L$ change $V_{out}$?
items.back: Connecting load $R_L$ to the tap puts it in parallel with $R_2$, so the effective bottom resistance becomes $R_2 \| R_L < R_2$, pulling $V_{out}$ below the unloaded value. Negligible only when $R_L \gg R_2$.

items.front: What is the Delta-Wye ($\Delta$-Y) transformation used for, and give the Y-resistor formula.
items.back: It converts three resistors in a triangle ($\Delta$/$\pi$) to three in a star (Y/T) to enable further series-parallel reduction in bridge meshes. A Y branch is $R_Y = \dfrac{R_a R_b}{R_a + R_b + R_c}$ (product of the two adjacent $\Delta$ resistors over the sum of all three).

items.prompt: A divider has $R_1 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $V_{in} = 10\,\text{V}$. A $10\,\text{k}\Omega$ load is attached to the tap. What is the loaded $V_{out}$?
items.explanation: $R_2 \| R_L = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$. Then $V_{out} = 10 \times \frac{5}{10 + 5} = 10 \times \frac{1}{3} = 3.33\,\text{V}$, well below the 5 V unloaded value — the loading effect.

items.prompt: Two resistors, $6\,\Omega$ and $3\,\Omega$, are in parallel. What is the equivalent resistance?
items.explanation: $R_{eq} = \frac{6 \times 3}{6 + 3} = \frac{18}{9} = 2\,\Omega$, which is smaller than the smaller resistor, as expected.

items.prompt: A 9 mA source feeds two parallel resistors $R_1 = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_2 = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$. How much current flows through $R_1$?
items.explanation: Current divider: $I_1 = I\,\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} = 9\,\text{mA} \times \frac{1}{2+1} = 3\,\text{mA}$. The smaller resistor ($R_2$) carries the larger share (6 mA).

items.prompt: When is the simple voltage-divider equation $V_{out} = V_{in} R_2/(R_1+R_2)$ accurate?
choices: When the load resistance is much greater than $R_2$
When the load resistance is much less than $R_2$
items.explanation: The unloaded formula assumes negligible current is drawn from the tap, i.e. $R_L \gg R_2$ so that $R_2 \| R_L \approx R_2$. A comparable or smaller load causes significant droop.

lessons.title: Superposition, Thevenin/Norton & Max Power Transfer
lessons.content:
The capstone tools of linear DC analysis let you replace arbitrarily complex networks with trivially simple ones. **Superposition** states that in a linear circuit the response (any voltage or current) equals the sum of the responses to each independent source acting alone — with the others *deactivated*: voltage sources shorted, current sources opened. Dependent sources stay active. It is conceptually clean but often more work than nodal/mesh; its real value is as the proof that the next two theorems exist.

**Thevenin's theorem** says any linear two-terminal network reduces to a single voltage source $V_{th}$ in series with a resistance $R_{th}$. $V_{th}$ is the **open-circuit voltage** at the terminals; $R_{th}$ is the resistance seen looking back with all independent sources deactivated. **Norton's theorem** is the dual: a current source $I_N = I_{sc}$ (short-circuit current) in parallel with $R_N = R_{th}$. The two are related by **source transformation**:

$$V_{th} = I_N R_{th}, \qquad R_{th} = \frac{V_{oc}}{I_{sc}}.$$

Thevenin is *the* mental model for "what does the rest of the circuit look like from here" — it reappears as amplifier input/output impedance and as matching-network thinking later.

Once a source is reduced to $V_{th}$ with $R_{th}$, the **maximum power transfer** theorem tells you the load that extracts the most power: $R_{load} = R_{th}$. At that match, delivered power peaks at

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_{th}^2}{4 R_{th}},$$

but efficiency is only 50% (half the power burns in $R_{th}$). That is why power systems instead aim for $R_{load} \gg R_{th}$ (maximum voltage transfer, high efficiency), while RF/signal front-ends deliberately match for maximum power.

items.front: State the superposition principle and how sources are deactivated.
items.back: In a linear circuit, the total response is the sum of responses to each independent source acting alone. To deactivate the others: short voltage sources (0 V) and open current sources (0 A). Dependent sources remain active throughout.
items.hint: One source at a time; short V, open I.

items.front: Define the Thevenin equivalent of a linear two-terminal network.
items.back: A single voltage source $V_{th}$ (the open-circuit terminal voltage) in series with $R_{th}$ (the resistance looking back into the terminals with all independent sources deactivated). It reproduces the network's behavior at those terminals exactly.

items.front: How are Thevenin and Norton equivalents related (source transformation)?
items.back: Norton uses $I_N = I_{sc}$ in parallel with $R_N = R_{th}$. They convert via $V_{th} = I_N R_{th}$, and the common resistance is $R_{th} = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{I_{sc}}$.

items.front: How do you find $R_{th}$ when the network contains only independent sources?
items.back: Deactivate all independent sources (short voltage sources, open current sources) and compute the equivalent resistance seen from the two terminals by series/parallel reduction. (With dependent sources present, instead apply a test source and take $R_{th} = v_{test}/i_{test}$.)

items.front: State the maximum power transfer condition and the resulting peak power.
items.back: Maximum power is delivered to the load when $R_{load} = R_{th}$. The peak is $P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{th}^2}{4 R_{th}}$, at only 50% efficiency (the other half dissipates in $R_{th}$).

items.front: Why do power-distribution systems NOT operate at the maximum-power-transfer match?
items.back: At the match ($R_{load} = R_{th}$) efficiency is only 50% — half the energy is wasted in the source resistance. Power systems want high efficiency, so they design for $R_{load} \gg R_{th}$ (maximum voltage transfer). Matching for max power is reserved for signal/RF front-ends.

items.prompt: A network has open-circuit terminal voltage 12 V and short-circuit terminal current 3 A. What is its Thevenin resistance?
items.explanation: $R_{th} = \frac{V_{oc}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{12}{3} = 4\,\Omega$. So the Thevenin equivalent is a 12 V source in series with $4\,\Omega$.

items.prompt: A source has $V_{th} = 10\,\text{V}$ and $R_{th} = 5\,\Omega$. What load resistance and maximum power can it deliver?
When applying superposition to find a current due to a 5 V source and a 2 A source, how do you treat them when considering the 5 V source alone?
choices: Keep the 5 V source; open the 2 A current source
Short the 5 V source; keep the 2 A source
Open the 5 V source; short the 2 A source
items.explanation: Considering the 5 V source acting alone, deactivate the other independent source: a current source is deactivated by opening it (0 A). The 5 V source remains in the circuit.

items.prompt: A Norton equivalent has $I_N = 2\,\text{A}$ and $R_N = 6\,\Omega$. The equivalent Thevenin voltage is:
items.explanation: Source transformation: $V_{th} = I_N R_N = 2 \times 6 = 12\,\text{V}$, in series with the same $6\,\Omega$.
